skill.name: nextjs-devtools
skill.description: Inspect and debug Next.js applications through an MCP client. Use it to query routes, components, build details, and Next.js configuration.
skill.summary: Query Next.js project routes, components, build data, and configuration through MCP.
skill.author: 92Bilal26

security_audit.summary: Static findings for weak cryptography and markdown backticks are false positives. The confirmed risks are shell-based command execution for stdio MCP servers and network calls to MCP endpoints. The skill appears development-focused, but publication should warn users about untrusted commands and unpinned npx packages.

medium_findings.title: Shell-Based Stdio Server Execution
medium_findings.description: The MCP client passes a user-provided stdio command to subprocess.Popen with shell=True. This is legitimate for MCP tooling, but unsafe if a prompt or user supplies untrusted command text.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call uses shell=True and receives args.stdio. The risk depends on whether command input is trusted.

medium_findings.title: Unpinned npx Package Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill documentation and server script run next-devtools-mcp through npx with @latest. This can execute newly published package code without version review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence repeatedly uses npx next-devtools-mcp@latest. That is a clear supply-chain risk, though not proof of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: User-Selected MCP Network Endpoint
low_findings.description: The HTTP transport sends JSON-RPC requests and optional headers to a user-provided MCP URL. This is expected behavior, but users should avoid untrusted endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly performs HTTP requests to a caller-supplied URL. No evidence shows forced exfiltration or a hardcoded external receiver.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography detections point to markdown, descriptions, argument parsing, and JSON formatting. No hashing or encryption code was found at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed lines contain markdown headings, descriptive strings, and JSON output. They do not implement cryptographic algorithms.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.Popen With shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: Runs a command string through the shell for stdio MCP servers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The call explicitly sets shell=True on a command string.

dangerous_patterns.title: Runtime Package Execution Through npx
dangerous_patterns.description: Executes the latest next-devtools-mcp package at runtime.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command uses npx with @latest, which can run package code fetched at execution time.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP Requests to Caller-Provided Endpoint
dangerous_patterns.description: Sends MCP JSON-RPC payloads and optional headers to the selected URL.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The HTTP client is explicit and user-controlled. No forced external destination was found.

content.value_statement: Next.js projects can be hard to inspect across routes, components, and build settings. This skill connects to a DevTools MCP server and returns focused project diagnostics.
actual_capabilities: Starts a Next.js DevTools MCP server through the included shell script.
Calls MCP tools over stdio or HTTP transports.
Lists available MCP tools from a connected server.
Requests route, component, build, and configuration information.
Emits MCP tool schemas as readable markdown or JSON.
limitations: Requires a Next.js project and the next-devtools-mcp package.
Does not provide general React analysis outside Next.js projects.
Runs local commands, so users must trust the selected MCP server command.
HTTP mode depends on an already reachable MCP endpoint.

use_cases.title: Route Debugging
use_cases.description: Inspect app routes and review details for a specific route during development.

use_cases.title: Build Configuration Review
use_cases.description: Check build information and Next.js configuration while investigating build issues.

use_cases.title: Project Structure Discovery
use_cases.description: List components and available MCP tools before changing an unfamiliar codebase.

prompt_templates.title: List Routes
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Next.js DevTools skill to list all routes in this project and summarize the route groups.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect One Route
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to inspect the /api/auth route and explain its available details in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Review Build Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to get build information and Next.js configuration, then identify likely build misconfigurations.

prompt_templates.title: Map Project Surface
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to list routes, components, and config, then create a concise project inspection report.

output_examples.input: List all routes in my Next.js project.
output: A route inventory grouped by app area.
Notes about dynamic routes and API routes.
A short summary of routing structure.
Build settings returned by the MCP server.

output_examples.input: Show components discovered by the DevTools server.
output: A component list from the connected project.

best_practices: Run the skill from the root of a trusted Next.js project.
Pin the MCP server package version before repeated team use.
Use local MCP endpoints unless you trust the remote server.
anti_patterns: Do not pass prompt-generated shell commands to the stdio option.
Do not send private project data to unknown MCP endpoints.
Do not use this skill for non-Next.js React projects.

faq.question: Does this skill require a Next.js project?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed for Next.js applications and depends on Next.js project structure.

faq.question: Can it inspect routes?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented MCP tools include route listing and route detail inspection.

faq.question: Can it inspect components?
faq.answer: Yes. It can call the component listing tool exposed by the DevTools MCP server.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: Yes. HTTP mode contacts a selected MCP endpoint, and npx may download the MCP server package.

faq.question: Is it safe to run arbitrary stdio commands?
faq.answer: No. Only run trusted MCP server commands because stdio mode uses shell execution.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
